reviews.review_text:
This game is a paradox. It's aged poorly, but still holds up. It's one of the best RPGs I've ever played, and it sucks as a game for playing a role. 

The reality is that it's probably too old and janky to be appealing to new players- it's from an era of gaming where you had to commit some time to learning HOW to play a game "correctly", and most players will be entirely turned off within minutes of walking into the starting town. To people who played this when it was still fresh, the scope of this game is still mind blowing decades later.

It was made as Bethesda's dying breath- they knew this was their last shot before they were going to have to close their doors, so they poured their hearts and souls into it, taking every risk imaginable to make one of the most incredible pieces of media to exist. 

It's my favorite game of all time, and I'd recommend it only to people who are willing to:
1. Respect the game rules. You will have a bad time if you give a fighter magic-focused attributes- be sensical.
2. Enjoy the game rules. A low-level fighter type isn't going to cut it in the mage's guild- be intentional.
3. Absolutely disrespect the game rules. Wait, I can enchant a sword to let me levitate? Mage's guild here I come!

You have to earn it, but the game DOES go from a hardcore RPG where class and gear choices matter into a total sandbox where anything is possible. I played one character who was a conjuration battlemage that liked to travel light. Just some robes and a ring or two, but as soon as combat started he would summon in heavy armor, a sword, a shield, and an ally to fight alongside him. It was a total blast. 

To the right person, Morrowind is an easy 11/10, but to most people this will be a 2/10 game. Which one are you?
mogs every elder scrolls game before and after
the graphics and combat is dated but, no game has ever created a setting even close to this one, absolute masterpiece.

I started my Elder Scrolls journey with Skyrim, and for literal years I refused to even consider playing Morrowind. It just looked too dated and unappealing. Eventually some Morrowind memes I saw online convinced me to give it a try. I wanted in on the references and jokes, and with the game being dirt cheap I figured why not.

The very beginning almost broke me- the starting speed was so agonizingly slow I wanted to bash my head against my monitor. In Morrowind, your speed is an actual stat you have to grind up through skills and attributes, so it starts brutally slow until you put in the time to level it. Instead of doing that, I opened up console, bumped my movement speed to a higher level and everything clicked after that. Now I'm zooming across the map, parkouring off buildings. That fluid, unrestricted movement is something I never knew I was craving in an Elder Scrolls game.

The story is such a vibe. No spoilers. All I will say is that it's strange, mysterious and complex. 

What a fantastic game. Play with OpenMW plus the Project Tamriel Rebuilt mods for a great experience full of content. And if you are bad with directions like me... include a quest marker mod as well.
Highly Recommended.
Last good game Bethesda made. Go install Project Tamriel and Tamriel Rebuilt.

This was my first ES game.

Played this game for sentimental reasons, as I owned it back on the OG Xbox. I never got around to finding and beating Dagoth Ur, so i seen it on sale and thought.. lets do it lol.

Games aged quite well tbf. but your talking to someone who would still happily go another run through of the OG FFVII.

So yeah, finaly beat the game and glad I did. The only negative if you want to call it that was remembering that the hit box levels up as you do which can be a bit frustrating. I do like the way the game makes you read to work things out, and theres no "real" fast travel that would take the size of the true game away.

Im on a bit of an ES binge now and beat OG oblivion too.

Would love to see a remake/remaster of Morrowind as they did for Oblivion.

I truly enjoyed my time with this masterpiece. An unforgettable RPG experience.

My friends and I had no idea what it was when we got it and we set up our TV's and consoles side by side and went at the game. So many nostalgic memories are from this game. My favorite is "What's hitting me?!?...... I am dead again.. That stupid flying thing got me again before I could see it"  =D   Followed by "I don't remember how I got to where I was...." Good times.... Would recommend for the experience as well as the "hidden" story lines. I still have the original GoT manual.... for Xbox...

Where do I even begin with this game?

Environment: 8.5/10
Swamplands and ashlands are most of what you'll find here. Definitely not as beautiful as Oblivion or Skyrim, but the atmosphere the environments give off make the world definitely worth exploring

Combat: 8/10
You'll swing and miss a lot starting out, which causes a lot of people to quit early on. Why? The mindset. Keep in mind this is an RPG in its truest sense. Your stamina and weapon proficiency play a big part. If it helps, think of this game more as fast "turn-based". You won't always land a hit on your turn, but it does give you more freedom than actual turn-based RPGs give.

Story: 10/10
There are TONS of books and documents scattered throughout the world that give you lore of not just the land you're in, but the whole world around you beyond things you'll never get to see, but know exists in the universe. You're destined to be the hero that saves the land, but how long that takes and how you choose to handle different quests is up to you.

Graphics: 5/10
This game released a long time ago, so don't expect anything groundbreaking

All in all this game will always be a classic and welcome entry in The Elder Scrolls.
Game is honestly amazing if you install openmw. I can see why people say this is the best elder scrolls game.

Morrowind is one of the best RPGs ever made, at least that is my opinion. It's a game which tests your knowledge of the Elder Scrolls universe, and your understanding of its lore (by reading in-game books); understanding enhances the story. I believe no other role playing game really gives you that experience, of living within the world and trying to unravel its mysteries through research and questing beyond the main story. As you play, the mystery of who the Nerevarine is is uncovered by YOU. You make the effort of figuring things out, the game does not do it for you. 
Your understanding of the game's systems--spell-making, enchanting, alchemy, leveling--will improve your gameplay, and turn the game into one of a somewhat slog into something exciting, something that truly gives you free reign over the world to transform it into what you want it to be. Create a spell with fortify 500 acrobatics and jump over entire towns; cast chameleon to become undefeatable in combat; create potions of light that nuke your entire screen. There are so many possibilities and every style of play is so much fun. Traverse Vvardenfell and take faction quests from various leaders of their respective districts, help a random lady find her ring and acquire one of the most powerful and ridiculous amulets in the game.
Morrowind is a game with so much to do, and the DLCs are even better. Morrowind is a game about knowledge, and Morrowind is a game about political intrigue. The world is full of depth, and the systems are equally so. If you can get past the extremely slow walk speed, the early combat which is essentially spamming until you connect, the early magic where you constantly fail to cast, etc.--I won't say the game is perfect, these mechanics are certainly outdated--you will have so much fun and have a unique experience which nothing else can provide. Play Morrowind at least once. I began at 14, it changed my life.

I stopped playing my steam copy after I started using OpenMW, so my playtime is probably closer to 250 than just 50 hours. Its an older title now and does take some getting used to. Its easy to make the game a breeze by playing optimally and minmaxxing but if you really do like the game you'll find yourself wanting to stay as weak as possible to fully immerse yourself as your character. I never really roleplay in my roleplaying games but Morrowind has always gotten me into the frame of mind my specific character would be in, whether it be planning supplies for a trip or deciding what quest rewards to keep I always think not as myself but as the character I play.

7 hours so far on Steam, 2000+ on the original Xbox. This is my favorite video game.

There's no game that has affected my love of storytelling or immersion like Morrowind. It was my gateway drug to fantasy, leading me to novels, films, and D&D. I love everything about the game, despite how much it shows its age. The world is unique and strange, and the story is magnificent. This is a game made by people who loved it and it shows.

Those were the days, before laptops and smartphones, before you could check YouTube for "lets plays". Good times.

It seems like a fun game and I was genuinely into it. However, I can not change the brightness settings. When I do, nothing happens and rebooting has availed me none. Wouldn't be a problem but interiors are VERY dark and if there are no candles or lanterns its pitch black. Really hard to navigate a cave this way. I wouldn't recommend simply due to this issue.

This is still one of bethesda greatest gems. It is one of the only open worlds with a true table top rpg class builders. Even though many hate chance rolls it really does add another layer to class building. Dodge chance, spell chance, and attributes really make the 3 domains of stealth, melee, and magic progress differently and there are things a character of that architype will have that the other never will. Like a max melee will have the most health since endurance grants more health the earilier you spec into it and you can wear the daedric armor set and not be encumbered. Sleath skills somewhat work when your character specs into it dodge chance is a good plus for high agility chacters, and mages can have an insane level of utility spells.

Unlike actual table tops though it still has the bethesda elder scroll formula of get better by doing. Even if character sucks at progressing a skill with low chance there is a chance for them to master everything. This is also the only elder scrolls games with real opposing factions like fallout. The games quests and lore are written on the same level they don't really dumb down the setting like in skyrim just to make the quests more accessible. The factions have real intrigue and ambitions only really seen since fallout new vegas for bethesda games writting. 

Fast travel is a good system. It costs money and there is also public transport between all the major settlements. There is even obilesk that teleport you as a reward for those who clear dungeons. The Roads, road signs, maps, journals, landmarks are actually important for completing your quests. The way a character can speed up non fast travel is very unique too. High Atheletics, high speed, high acrobatics, potions that boost movement, scrolls that boost movement, enchanted items you make that let you fly, casting spells like water walk, fly, water breath, and mark teleport adds an insane choice of traversal. Money is also a key resource in this game enchanting, potions, and fast travel all cost money. Money is also integral for bribes and training. Many of these systems are unique to morrowind and you find it in any other bethesda game. 

The character models are a bit dated but the world and building textures are still great at showing you different cultures in this game. I reccomend playing with higher res texture mods. Some dark elves live in stucco adobe, others giant mushrooms, other basic wood and stone structure, and the Elites on the coast live in ziggurots above the water. The humans also have a disinct style with a medieval fortress and cottages. The capital ebonheart is a human colony with dark elve elites living there. The native dark elves called ashlanders are nomads that live in tents in ash lands and the beast races live in shacks on marshy platations. The world shows so many race and class without having to look at a wiki. It hard to even generalize how divided the elite dark elves are too. It is a weirdly bethesda open world strength.

I'm REALLY trying to like at least one Bethesda game and this one is sadly no different to all others. The heart of gameplay for an open-world RPG are the quests. Here, every quest dumps a ton of unmemorable names onto you while providing absolutely no sense of direction or progression. Why should I care? Why should I bother with any of the (mostly) fetch quests? None of them are intrigueing, none of the areas are interesting (it's just a wavy terrain with towns and forts scattered about) NOTHING makes me want to play the game. Maybe at the time this was the best RPG there was but today it utterly dissapoints.

[h1] Dagoth Ur was correct in every single way and vindicated by both TES IV: Oblivion and TES V: Skyrim as well as in THIS VERY GAME. [/h1]

*Hits skooma pipe*


The Tribunal's power being subject to nothing more than "Might makes right" in their treacherous dealings means they themselves have no legitimate right to their power, even less so given that despite their administrative position as "Living Gods," VEHK and SEHT took little interest in anything other than personal cultivation, revealed by the former's vocal exasperation and the latter's seclusion, leaving AYEM and Great House councilors to rule. Dagoth Ur was the only one willing to fulfill the social contract and act to the benefit of his people.

Dagoth Ur expressed no desire to convert all of the population to sleepers, and those that were showed no signs of discomfort if they were chosen by Dagoth Ur, and instead gained immense knowledge and power which offered them a fulfilling life due to their direct contact with divinity. I have no sympathy for outlanders who were afflicted by Corprus and found themselves unable to receive its true power.

*swigs flin*
Futhermore, much like any real-life nation-state, the Empire was revealed as an ineffectual protective force which only sought to use the Dunmeri people as a tax base.
Oblivion reveals to us the impotence of the Empire to stop would-be assassins from eliminating their ranks and an inability to mount a counteroffensive to the Oblivion crisis, with Skyrim and later novelization revealing the Red Year (A horrible writing choice, by the way) and the Empire all but abandoning them during relief efforts and invasion by Black Marsh.

*huffs moon sugar*
[h3] Dagoth Ur could have stopped all of this. [/h3]
Being a living God, he could have given TRUE structure to the dunmer people,  repelled Mehrunes Dagon, and SINGLEHANDEDLY kept Baar Dau afloat and kept Morrowind as a homeland for its people, all of these are revealed as partof his plan.
Driving out the tribunal, and the empire, and establishing a divine connection for his People would have been far better than the fate Morrowind and more broadly Tamriel were faced with during the events of TES:IV and TES:V. Afflicting the land with Divine Knowledge would cause suffering, sure, but it would induce immortality itself in the faithful and offer them a divine connection theorized by Taoist scholars and postulated as the goal of all life by Laotzi in our own world.

Dagoth Ur. My muse. My hero. My love. Head of the only Great House which loved and cherished its people not as subjects but as adored sons and daughters. The heroes of the Sixth House shall remain unmourned, but not by I. I shall weep for your sacrifice forever. I shall weep for your ideals. I shall weep for your dream of a world connected to heaven. Of Man liberated from their inability to lead themselves.

I shall forever weep for you, my most beloved Dagoth Ur...
